VO_EN: You arrive second.
The gloves go on before you step out of the car.
Every time.
Thirty-seven scenes.
Not one investigation.
Not one question.
By the time you leave, it never happened.
First week on patrol.
Night shift. January. The streets smell like wet concrete.
You pull up to a scene and there are already four cruisers there.
Lights still going. Nobody's rushing.
You watch a cop walk to the body.
He kicks the gun two feet closer to the right hand.
Doesn't bend down. Doesn't look around.
Just kicks it. Like clearing a rock off a sidewalk.
Nobody reacts.
You say nothing.
That is the first clean.
Silence.
You learn it before you learn anything else.
Three days later a senior detective pulls you aside after shift.
Parking lot. His jacket's wrinkled. Tie hanging loose.
He smells like cigarettes and burned coffee.
He doesn't teach you how to read a crime scene.
He teaches you how to read a problem.
Where the story falls apart.
What the camera caught that shouldn't be in the report.
He says it like he's giving you directions to the gas station.
Turn left here. Turn right there. Don't go straight.
You watch him work a scene for the first time two weeks in.
His hands move and your eyes follow.
He tilts the barrel three degrees. Maybe four.
Checks the sightline from where the dash cam was parked.
Writes two words in his notebook. Closes it.
The investigation closes in a week. Justified shooting.
You haven't touched anything.
You haven't moved a thing.
But you know how it works now.
You drive home that night.
You sit in the car a while. Radio off. Engine running.
You don't touch anything.
But you don't report it either.
Your first clean.
Three months in. February maybe. Or March.
The scene is simple. One body. One officer.
One story that doesn't match.
The hands are up. Palms open.
That is not what self-defense looks like.
You kneel down. The asphalt's cold through your pants.
The latex squeaks when you pull the gloves tight.
You reposition the hands.
One on the waistband. One reaching.
Four seconds. You count them.
Four seconds and the scene tells a different story.
You find the officer in the hallway by the vending machine.
His shirt's untucked. Hands won't stop moving.
You lean against the wall.
Tell me again.
He starts over. You stop him at the second sentence.
Not from when it started. From when you arrived.
Tell me again.
Third time the story holds.
It always takes three.
You don't know why it's always three.
You file the report at the precinct.
Scene cleared by midnight.
You drive home. Park in the driveway.
You sit there for a while. Headlights off. Engine ticking.
You wait for someone to call.
For a voice that says something doesn't add up.
You check your phone twice.
No one calls.
No one questions it.
By the time you leave, it never happened.
You step back.
You look at the scene.
You check if it matches the story you built.
It does.
You did that.
The gloves go on slower now.
One finger at a time. Like putting on a second skin.
You reach for the radio and turn it down.
Dispatch doesn't need to hear this part.
It gets easier.
That's the part nobody tells you.
Next scene. The gun position says self-defense.
But the casings are wrong.
Scattered in a line. Shooter was moving backward.
You can fix the gun or you can fix the casings.
Not both.
You choose the gun.
You always choose the gun.
You fix the gun.
You leave the casings.
You tell yourself it won't matter.
It always matters later.
Two inches.
That's all it takes.
Two inches turns an execution into self-defense.
Thirty-seven scenes.
Not one question.
Not one correction.
Not one version that wasn't yours.
The autopsy comes back three weeks later.
Bruising pattern doesn't match the hand position.
Not the position in your photos anyway.
You call the officer at home.
Were the hands up or down when you got there.
Long pause.
Down.
No.
Up. I think up.
You change one line in the report.
Hands were observed reaching toward waistband.
One line changes everything.
Next month. Different scene.
The body camera was on when you got there.
You pull the battery. The red light blinks twice then goes dark.
But the sync light was on first.
Cloud upload. You don't know how much got through.
You don't know if the casings from last time will come back.
You clean the scene anyway.
Because the report will reach them first.
Tell me again.
You say it like a director giving notes now.
Skip the part about the hands.
They weren't up.
They were reaching.
The officer nods. He believes it now. Or he's learned to.
Drop gun in the trunk. Where it always is.
Camera log reads equipment malfunction.
You fill it out without looking at the form.
There's a scratch on the desk. A long one. You follow it with your finger.
You step back.
It already matches.
You knew it would.
Your hands know the work before your brain catches up.
You stop remembering names.
First you remember faces. Then only charges.
Then just case numbers.
You don't remember when that changed.
You stop asking what happened.
You ask what it needs to look like.
New call.
You arrive. Lights still spinning on the cruisers.
You don't wait for the briefing.
You don't ask what happened.
You start working.
You move the gun.
You pocket a casing.
You reposition the hands.
Then you stop.
You read the scene.
The shooting was justified.
The scene was clean.
Everything was where it should've been.
You already changed three things.
You try to put the gun back where it was.
The angle's wrong.
But not the way you expected.
It's already been moved.
Someone got here before you.
Or you were here before and don't remember.
You don't know which is worse.
You look down at your hands.
Gloves on.
You don't remember putting them on.
You don't remember walking to the body.
You don't remember starting.
Back at the desk. You pull up an old report.
Your handwriting.
Hands reaching toward waistband.
You remember reaching.
You don't remember open.
You don't remember up.
Because you wrote reaching.
And reaching is what you remember now.
By the time you leave, it never happened.
You write the report anyway. Same form. Same pen.
The report matches what you made.
Not what was there.
You don't know what was there anymore.
A new cop starts riding along the next year.
Quiet kid. Keeps his hands in his pockets.
He watches you work a scene.
Eyes on your hands the whole time.
Doesn't say anything. Doesn't ask.
Next month he moves a casing on his own.
Doesn't announce it. Just does it.
Looks at you.
You nod.
Someone asks him what he saw that night.
I arrived after.
Your words. Not his.
Six months later you hear him say it to someone else.
Tell me again.
Your phrase. Your cadence.
You watch him work.
The way you do.
Exactly the way you do.
You don't remember learning it either.
They don't remember asking.
By the time you leave, it never happened.
You step back.
You look at the scene.
It looks right.
You don't know why.
You pull up a report from last year. Your handwriting.
Every detail precise.
You can't tell which details are real.
Phone rings.
Same voice. Same fear.
Same silence before they tell you what happened.
You're already reaching for the gloves.
You don't ask what happened.
You haven't asked in years.
It doesn't matter what happened.
Only what it needs to look like.
You arrive second.
You always do.
You don't remember what it was anymore.
